speaker: interviewer
text: --Brand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you change the camera, the position of the camera?

speaker: interviewee
text: Interviewer.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about the Germans coming into Brody and how the Jewish life changed. Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they started to-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they made the Jews move to a certain-- the old, dilapidated part of Brody. They put in a wire-- wire-- a wire fence and guards. And that's it. That was the ghetto. And the-- the Jews, of course, were assigned by the Judenrat a room. Instead of apartment, a room, a room with a kitchen, a room without a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the Jews notified that they are going to be moved to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a law-- a-- a announcement. The Gestapo, right away-- there was an all over-- there was announcements, by this and this day. You had three days to move. Take only your hand luggage.

speaker: interviewer
text: By then, you were already identified by-- by the yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wasn't yet. And I-- I-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I-- I wasn't there. I was-- I was be-- I left Brody, myself, personally, before the Jews had to move to the ghetto. A very interesting story. I was living in-- I-- we were moved, because we were bombed out twice. And I remember, on the third floor, in-- in our old building, we still-- my mother still cooked meals. My father walked around, didn't have nothing to do. I was reading books. I walking around.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the German invasion or before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the German-- after the German invasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you identified by stars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. I had a Magen David. And then, I walk around on the street. First of all, they grabbed me. There was a-- suddenly, I walk on the main street, Gold Strasse, and there was a truck and-- and German soldiers. And they're-- they're going, go in the truck. I go in the truck. There were Jew-- whoever they put, mostly Jewish people. We were about 10 or 12.

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? Outside Brody, on the-- on the outskirts of Brody, Poland built new military barracks, brand new, the best. Then the-- then the Poland was disappeared. And the barracks were dirty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Germans wanted to clean the barracks for-- for the-- for the German army. So they needed people to clean the barracks. They'll take anybody on the street. They grabbed. And there were several trucks on different streets. Anyway, they brought us in the yard, big yard, exercise yard, military-- military barracks. And by the time-- and they-- and we are standing, unorganized, without anybody telling us what to do, without tools. And in the barracks, there, I walked around to the barracks, straw and the garbage and everything. I walk out there. We had a Jewish boy, who was mentally deficient. They grabbed him, too. And one soldier says-- told him, got to the right. And he was-- he's-- it was some really bad-- He walked around dirty. The-- the parents didn't take good care of him. He didn't listen. So from a distance, he shot him. Just took the-- the gun and killed him, just like that. And the barracks were built right by the forest, right next to the forest, outside of town. So, hey, I don't like it, I told myself. I don't like it. I don't want to work for them. And I have to see how I can get out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went in the back. We went in the forest. And I walked about a half a mile. Then I backtracked, and I came home. That's all. Nobody kept any track of me. They was not-- not organized.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I-- I-- I talked to the-- I go out, walk on the street, a couple days later. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And yeah, meantime, I got a job. They needed a doctor, because they had Russian prisoners of war, sick Russian prisoners of war. Out of town, sick Russian prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I reported myself. I'll go work for the-- they give me a meal. That's all. It was out of town, also. So I carried a-- a-- a-- a-- a little light jacket and covered it up, my-- my Magen David.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because, you know, if they see Magen David, no matter what, you go. You never know what can happen to you. So you just cover it up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walked about a mile and a half. And there were Russian soldiers, on-- on straw, lay-- laying around-- lying around, sick. Some of them were wounded. Some of them were malnourished, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they got, you know, vegetables, soup, you know, a piece of bread. And they had a horse to deliver [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] food, you know, groceries, to this. And I was treating them. And there were two other doctors. We didn't do anything just see that they get nourishment. We didn't do anything. And one day, I remember, one soldier-- and one Russian prisoners of war developed acute appendicitis. An so a-- a-- a miner, a Russian coal miner operated on him. A Russian coal miner operated, did the-- an appendectomy. And this-- just gave him dope, vodka. And the Russian solider didn't say a word.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't nobody survive. Because we were in there about three, four weeks. And then one day, I came to work, and he says, no more-- no more sick. It's locked up. They took them all in the forest. They killed them. All there, they were about 100, 100 patients. They killed them all. That's it. Gestapo--

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: --when they-- the Gestapo got involved. As long as the-- the army treated them. When the Gestapo took over, they killed them. So I didn't have nowhere to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I walk around the streets, again, in Brody. Walk here, walk there. And say, what do you know? Did you hear anything? What's going on? The ghetto was not organized yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I meet a friend of mine. And he's from a different, little town, a doctor, who graduated in Italy, Stickler, I remember. So he was my age.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, how you doing? He says-- he asks me, first of all, do I have a cigarette? I say, sure, I have a cigarette. Give me a cigarette. See, you will be surprised. There is a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was working in a camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working in a different camp when they were building the road. He was a doctor in-- in Ponikwa. There was a-- a palace of a Polish count, who was-- used to be-- who owned thousands of acres of land. He had a little palace. By the time the war came out, he didn't have no land, because he was-- he liked to sit in Nice, in-- in-- in the-- in-- in southern France and play poker. So he lost everything. And the-- they-- and the Russians, they put the prisoners of war in the palace. And he was the doctor. This Stickler was the doctor. Neither-- you know, they were working on the highway. Everyday, go to work. And this-- this-- and then [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] they had-- they-- they-- the-- the-- once, one farmer-- and they didn't have any medical care, the village of Ponikwa. So anybody got sick, they called the doctor from the camp, Stickler. He was treating them, the farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, called-- they called. A man got the pneumonia in the village. They called farmer-- they called Stickler. So he came in, put in-- what do you call in English, pijawkas, the one that sucked blood? Leech. Leeches.

speaker: interviewee
text: Leeches! Next day, the guy got sick-- well. The temperature came down. He was fine. All right. So then the-- the-- the Russians ran away. That's it. The Germans are in town. I meet Stickler in the street. He says, they're looking for a doctor. And the-- the Germans are looking for a doctor in Ponikwa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And guess who is the-- they appointed the city manage-- the-- the village headman? They appointed the doctor who was-- had pneumonia-- who-- They appointed the farmer, who had ammonia, and who was cured by Stickler. And he's put in a request. He wants Stickler for the doctor there. Why they want a doctor, the Germans? Because there is epidemic of typhus fever-- typhus fever.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, so they-- so I-- they wanted him to go to be a doctor in this village. But his wife didn't want to let him go. And his father-in-law didn't let him go. Because it was a Ukrainian village.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was afraid. Ukrainians will kill Jews as-- as gladly as the Germans. So he can't go. So I told him, I'll go for you, without thinking, saying, OK. Because his father-- father-in-law was a pharmacist. He-- he lived in a tiny town all-- by-- outside-- on the other side of a forest. He thought maybe we will-- they will survive. You know, there was not much traffic, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I come home. I say, I'm going to be a doctor in-- in Ponikwa. I'll supply you with food. And I'll look out for you all, my mother and father. So my mother says, go. We will manage somehow. No, I'm not going without your permission. [SOB]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got a-- a wagon. And I came into Ponikwa, I-- with-- with a suitcase. And there was a medical center from the Russian times in-- in Ponikwa, in this village.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the doctor, during the Russian times, the doctor, who was the doctor in Ponikwa, is from Warsaw, a Jewish boy. He was a friend of Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] son, who was a medical student. It was a wonderful family. He had a car. He had a car. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] To make the story short, the Ukrainians killed him when the Germans came. They killed a Jew, right there. And then, when I came to Ponikwa, I took over this medical-- this office, a separate little building, with instruments, with-- with a couch, with everything, but no medicine.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- and I lived in the village, in the house of the-- of the headman, of the-- you know, what do you call it, the head of the city hall? Mayor?

speaker: interviewee
text: The mayor. I forget. Mayor. And I-- and-- and then the Germans started, already, a retreat from Russia. That was 1944. And I was there in the-- in this office, the whole day. And farmers came from different villages. There was not such a thing as a doctor in every little town, in every village. There was not such a thing. And they came in. They heard a doctor. Oh, my daughter is sick, my child is sick, my diarrhea, pneumonia, and this, and that. And I did the best I could. But I didn't take any money. Because you couldn't buy nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to stop you for a minute and go back. What happened to your parents in Brody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm coming to it. I'm coming to it. My parents are in the ghetto-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940-- 1943, '42, '43. Because they invaded Poland in 1941. 1943, yeah. And I didn't take any money from my patients. I had some camphor oil injections, this and that, you know. There was no medicine. We didn't have any medicine, much medicine. I made a diagnosis. I say, give him like a-- there was a midwife. And she was my mentor. She delivered babies for 35 years, 40 years. She never had an infection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a farmer woman. She had a thermometer. And she-- I am very friendly with midwives, because I'll tell you later. And she gave me a thermometer and a stethoscope. It's wooden stethoscope.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were talking. She came for a-- for a chat. And she brought me some goodies, bread, you know? I didn't-- I wasn't hungry, because-- Did she know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. And then, so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was a-- there was epidemic of typhus fever. And the priest was Greek-- Greek Catholic [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Church. The priest had two sons. One was a dumbbell. One was a genius. And this-- both of them went to-- together, with me, to-- in high-- to high school. The dumbbell was a dumbbell. The-- the-- the-- the genius became a professor of mathematics in University of Lviv, eventually.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was come-- when I-- and-- and when I was in the intermission, about this, after insert a-- a little detail.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in medical school, a-- a-- a Jewish man, who had a restaurant in Brody, decided to build a-- a-- a restaurant and an entertainment spot for dancing on the-- on the beach. There was a-- there was a-- a little lake. And then summertime, I had a orchestra of my own, five-- five people. We played there, for dinner, 5 o'clock, and-- and in the evening till the guests came in. And we-- every Saturday and Sunday, we had a lot of people. And there was a brewery, in the village, belonging to the count. And there was beer all the time. The priest came, and I got acquainted with everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What instrument did you play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Violin. Violin. We had a piano, violin, drums, and a clarinet. And every-- every Saturday-- And-- and he built this restaurant. And they paid me 5 zloty. That was $1 at that time. A evening, everybody got $1. But 5 zloty was a lot of money, because a dinner, in a restaurant, cost $0.25.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came, I became their doctor. Right away, I got acquainted with the-- I-- I-- I knew the boys, because the-- the one went to Polytechnical University in-- in Lviv. And he became, eventually, a professor. And the other one, I don't know what it was. He went also, but he didn't make very well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was coming there as a house guest. And I knew, I-- when I was playing-- I was playing the violin and the-- in that. And I came to visit, because the son was a friend-- was a-- he went to school with me. And this old priest-- and that they gave me a permission to be in-- and-- and to-- to stay as a Jewish doctor-- you saw the-- the ticket-- for a month. And every month, this old priest had to go to Brody, to the Bezirkshauptmann, to get another permission, a permit for me--

speaker: interviewer
text: For you?

speaker: interviewee
text: --for me to stay. That's all. And then, the 20th of May had come. That's when they liquidated the ghetto, 1944-- in 19-- 194-- let me see-- In 1943, they liquidated the ghetto. In other words, the ghetto was organized in 1942. 1943, they liquidated the ghetto or Judenrein, no Jews. And I got this message, because farmers are going there to market. And I knew all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They're coming there. Did you hear? They're coming. In the afternoon, I walked around. Did you hear? There are no Jews in-- in Brody anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I did-- so my-- I came home. I didn't say nothing to the landlady. I was like a member of the family there. They had-- they had a house, which composed of two parts. In one part, the old folks lived, the parents of the mayor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and-- and the-- and the right side, there was the mayor, his wife, and two little children. The mayor slept in one bed. And-- and-- and then we had a-- one room, a big room, comfortable. I slept in one bed in the big room. The mayor slept in the other bed in the big room. And the rest of the family slept in the kitchen. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I didn't go. Yeah. There-- when I was a doctor, and I had the office, and there was epidemic of typhus fever, the-- the Germans gave a order. Anybody gets sick in-- of typhus fever has to be transported to Brody, to the hospital. Because they're afraid they'll-- the-- the army will get typhus fever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So they transport everybody to the-- to-- all the sick to the hospital. And then-- but they didn't go to the hospital. They died. Everybody died. I don't know if they went to the hospital or they-- then they picked them up from the hospital. They went straight to the ditches and killed them all.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, a woman come in from a different village, on the other side of the-- of the little lake. That's where there was the Roman Catholic [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Church. And they were a different county, a different village. And she came in, was crying. And I had to report everybody to the-- to the Ukrainian authorities that is sick. Which I didn't see everybody. And so they-- they come sick, fever, to Brody, that's all. That's the Germans. And one woman come in and go down on her knees to me in this-- in the office.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, my daughter is sick. My daughter has typhus. My daughter has a very, very high fever. She's hallucinating. Would you please come?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I go visiting. They gave me a bowl of soup. I look at her, she has typhus fever. She has a rash and high fever. She goes around, and she goes down again on-- on-- on the knees. And she says, don't send her to the hospital. Says-- I didn't say a word. I say, OK. [SOB]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I didn't send her to the hospital. I told her-- I'll tell you, I have 30 aspirins. I saved it for myself. I'll give her 20 aspirins. Give her aspirins, sponge her off with cold water, give her a lot of water, cold liquids to drink, feed her liquids.

speaker: interviewee
text: The girl made it. I didn't report. Beautiful girl, 17-year-old girl, beautiful-- Olga. [SOB] And she said-- then she came to office about a week later. And the girl was fine. Nobody knew that she's sick. I didn't report it. She didn't let anybody to-- in the house. She says, kiss my hand. [SOB] [SOBBING] I can't control this. [CRYING] And she said, you're in trouble. Come to our house. We'll try to save your life. [CRYING] When the-- on the 20th of May, they liquidated the ghetto. I didn't tell where I lived. I didn't tell them nothing. I walked out at night, because that has to be very secret. I told her, I said, I'm going out for a little walk after supper. I got out. I didn't take nothing with me. I just walked around. It was in-- it-- it was in May. It was nice weather. I go for a walk around the lake. Go to go the other village. And she told me, knock on the window. We have-- we have somebody, a stranger or a guest, you go to the barn. And I'll pick you up. Sure enough, the mayor was there visiting of the other village. I knocked and-- and she came out. She said, go to the barn. I go to the barn to wait an hour. Then she came and picked me up. And I stayed there till the Russians came in 1944, in March, 1944. I stayed there. And I-- I-- and she-- she fed me. And I slept in this house, in this big bed. And she-- and the mayor slept in the other bed. And the old folks were in the other side of the-- the house. And I was like a member of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what happened to your family? Were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: 20th of May, they were killed. I was going through the digression. I cannot. I have a little jumbled up my brain. So forgive me, because I-- I can't backtrack. I have to backtrack. I was in this village taking care of patients. I made a friend, who was a millwright. There was a-- a-- a flour mill. I collected food for my parents, anything not perishable, bread, butter. But I kept it in-- in a cold place, meat, anything I could save, fruit. And every so often, this millwright says-- I say, I need somebody to-- to deliver my food to my parents in the ghetto. He said, sure, tomorrow. A Ukrainian fellow, nice guy, nice guy. And we load-- I loaded a sack of food. I go with him. And-- and he gave me such a coat, which they use when there is a storm, with a-- with a-- with a caboose, you know, a cover, and all the way. When it's 30 below 0, you know, and you have to go working in the woods, that's what you wear there. And I walked into the ghetto. And I-- I visited with my mother and my father. And I brought a-- on-- on my shoulders. He-- he had to stay outside the ghetto. He had a wagon and horses. I took the-- the sack of food and carry it and walk into this apartment. The first time I walked in, I thought I'll pass out. One room, the grass grows from the-- between the boards of the-- of the-- of the floor. My mother is fading. My father is very sick, not bedridden. But she cooked. My father was depressed. He was crying. It was terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I come out. And I talk to this millwright. He was in the marketplace. I had to walk a little. [SOBBING] I says, I don't know what to do with my parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He says, I can save one, mother or father, but I can't take both of them. [SOB] So I go back. And I tell them. Well, my father tells my mother, you go. You go. So no, you go. And she says, you go. And I said, I can't sit here forever. I got to go. Anyway, I took my mother. Father didn't want to go, absolutely not. And I managed. I brought the food. I say, I bring you food, again, again. Because you know, he says, anytime you need me, food. He wasn't hungry. People are dying from hunger, because they're sending people on the-- by the fence. If you have a gold watch, a ring, sometimes they give you food. But how many people have gold watches or gold rings or gold earrings or money or whatever. Eventually, everybody runs out of money. You have no money, you have to die from starvation. Because the only productive part of the society is the farmer in such a situation. Anyway, I took out my mother from the ghetto. And put her-- we-- we traveling on this wagon-- in this wagon. And I come to their house. And I kiss my mother on top-- [CRYING] [SOBBING] And he says, OK. Don't worry. We're all right. You-- we'll feed you. And I go back. I'll be here tomorrow. She is there. And she's depressed. [SOB] Three days later-- four days later, the farmer comes into the-- to my-- to my office says, she wants to go back [SOBBING] to the ghetto, stay with your father. She wants to die with your father, she says.

speaker: interviewee
text: So [SOBBING] I go there. [SOB] We had a big session. I don't know. [SOBBING] The farmer was crying. [CRYING] The father-- farmer was crying, everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOB] Took her back. That's it. I never saw her again. I never saw them again. [SOBBING] 20th of May, liquidated. Took them to the woods, everyone. That's their technique. And the-- the Ukrainian police helped them. And the Polish fire department helped them. And the Polish fire-- firemen carry a-- a hatchet. You know, if they go to a-- a-- a small hatchet. You know what is a hatchet. They go to, when there's a fire, to break up, when something is in the way. You know, to take care of the fire, they break up, with the hatchet, the wooden structures. And if that-- the Jews didn't want to go, they break their feet with the-- with the hatchet. That's the Polish-- Polish. And Ukrainians had guns. It was the militsiya, the police, with the guns, with everything. That's the-- the end of it. And the 20th of May, that was the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop again for a few minutes.
